title: Roots and Powers of Complex Numbers

epigraph.text: Be fruitful and multiply and fill the earth.
epigraph.quote: Every polynomial equation of degree $n$ has exactly $n$ roots, counted with multiplicity, in the complex numbers.

content.text:
The polar form makes computing powers and roots of complex numbers 
surprisingly elegant. We develop De Moivre's Theorem and use it to find 
all $n$th roots of unity.

content.title: De Moivre's Theorem
content.content:
For any real number $\theta$ and any integer $n$,
\[
 (\cos\theta + i\sin\theta)^n = \cos(n\theta) + i\sin(n\theta).
\]
Equivalently, $(e^{i\theta})^n = e^{in\theta}$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
For non-negative integers, we proceed by induction. The base case $n = 0$ 
gives $1 = 1$. For the inductive step:
$$
 (\cos\theta + i\sin\theta)^{n+1} 
 &= (\cos\theta + i\sin\theta)^n \cdot (\cos\theta + i\sin\theta) \\
 &= (\cos(n\theta) + i\sin(n\theta))(\cos\theta + i\sin\theta) \\
 &= \cos((n+1)\theta) + i\sin((n+1)\theta)
$$
by the angle addition formulas. For negative integers, use 
$z^{-n} = 1/z^n$.

content.title: Computing powers
content.solution:
First, write $1 + i$ in polar form: $|1 + i| = \sqrt{2}$ and 
$\arg(1 + i) = \pi/4$. So $1 + i = \sqrt{2}e^{i\pi/4}$.

By De Moivre's Theorem:
\[
 (1 + i)^{10} = (\sqrt{2})^{10} e^{i \cdot 10 \cdot \pi/4} 
 = 32 e^{i \cdot 5\pi/2} = 32 e^{i\pi/2} = 32i.
\]

content.text: We now solve the equation $w^n = z$ for $w$, given a complex number $z$.

content.title: $n$th Roots
content.content:
Let $z = re^{i\theta}$ be a nonzero complex number and $n$ a positive 
integer. The equation $w^n = z$ has exactly $n$ solutions, given by
\[
 w_k = r^{1/n} \exp\left(i\frac{\theta + 2\pi k}{n}\right), 
 \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n-1.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
If $w = se^{i\phi}$, then $w^n = s^n e^{in\phi} = re^{i\theta}$. Comparing 
moduli gives $s^n = r$, so $s = r^{1/n}$ (the unique positive $n$th root). 
Comparing arguments gives $n\phi = \theta + 2\pi k$ for some integer $k$, 
so $\phi = (\theta + 2\pi k)/n$. The values $k = 0, 1, \ldots, n-1$ give 
distinct roots; other integers repeat these.

content.text:
The $n$ roots are equally spaced around a circle of radius $r^{1/n}$, 
separated by angles of $2\pi/n$.
The $n$th roots of $1$ are called the **$n$th roots of unity**.

content.title: Primitive Root of Unity
content.content:
Set $\omega_n = e^{2\pi i/n}$. This is a **primitive $n$th root of 
unity**. The $n$th roots of unity are 
$1, \omega_n, \omega_n^2, \ldots, \omega_n^{n-1}$.

content.title: Sum of Roots of Unity
content.content:
The sum of the $n$th roots of unity is zero:
\[
 1 + \omega_n + \omega_n^2 + \cdots + \omega_n^{n-1} = 0.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Carl Friedrich Gauss (1777--1855)
content.content:
At age 19, Gauss proved that a regular 17-gon can be constructed with compass 
and straightedge, settling a question open since antiquity. His proof used 
the deep properties of 17th roots of unity. This discovery convinced him to 
pursue mathematics rather than philology.

marginNotes.text:
Where were you when I laid the foundation of 
the earth? Tell me, if you have understanding. Who determined its 
measurements—surely you know!

marginNotes.author: Abraham de Moivre (1707)
marginNotes.text:
The formula which gives all the 
powers and roots of any binomial.
